did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed automated melanoma diagnosing systems using machine learning techniques. Specifically, it employed Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers and feature selection methods (SVMRFE, Information Gain, Correlation-based Feature Subset Selection) to classify dermoscopic skin lesion images as malignant or benign based on extracted shape, texture, and color features.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing two new automated melanoma diagnosing systems using machine learning (SVM classifiers) and feature selection methods on a dataset of 102 dermoscopic images. It reports new results, including specific f-measures, sensitivity, and accuracy metrics, and compares these new systems against prior clinical and automated methods. Although published in a conference proceedings (2015 International Conference on Healthcare Informatics), it contains original research findings rather than being a review of existing literature.

title: New Accurate Automated Melanoma Diagnosing Systems
brief_summary: This study developed two automated melanoma diagnosing systems using Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers and feature selection methods on a dataset of 102 dermoscopic lesion images (52 malignant, 50 benign). The researchers extracted shape, texture, and color features, including a novel quantification of dots and globules, and evaluated subsets using Model, leave-one-out (LOO), and 10-fold cross-validation (10xCV) statistics. The first system utilized six features (6LFs) selected from ten, while the second system utilized five features (5LFs) selected from eleven by adding the dot/globule feature. The 5LFs system achieved the highest consistent f-measure across all validation statistics, with 10xCV accuracy of 86.9% and sensitivity of 88.68%. Comparison with clinical scoring systems and other automated methods demonstrated that the proposed systems offer superior or comparable accuracy and sensitivity for melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies dermoscopic images as malignant (melanoma) or benign. The dataset contained 52 malignant images out of 102 total. The systems were developed to improve melanoma diagnosis.
The dataset contained 50 benign images out of 102 total. These served as the negative class for the binary classification task against melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of dermoscopic skin lesions as malignant (melanoma) or benign
all_task_types.details: The paper develops automated melanoma diagnosing systems using Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers to predict discrete diagnostic categories (malignant vs. benign) based on extracted shape, texture, and color features. The task is explicitly defined as 'skin lesion classification' and 'melanoma diagnosis' with binary outcomes.

datasets_used.name: Dermoscopy: A Tutorial Dataset (Argenziano et al., 2000)
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopic skin lesion images sourced from the book 'Dermoscopy: A Tutorial' by Argenziano et al. [17]. It contains ground truth diagnoses for melanoma and benign lesions, including specific annotations for dots and globules (absent, present regular, present irregular) established by dermatologists and a general physician.

use.training: Used to train Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers via Model, Leave-One-Out (LOO), and 10-fold Cross-Validation (10xCV) statistics.
use.validation: Used for validation via LOO and 10xCV statistics to calculate f-measures for feature selection and system evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing via LOO and 10xCV statistics to evaluate the final diagnosing systems (6LFs and 5LFs).
use.other: Ground truth data for dots and globules from 81 images in this dataset was used to tune parameters for the automatic extraction of dots and globules in the remaining 21 images.

statistical_methods_used.method: Support Vector Machine Recursive Feature Elimination (SVMRFE)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Feature selection to rank lesion features by significance for melanoma diagnosis.
Feature selection to rank lesion features by significance for melanoma diagnosis.
Feature selection to identify significant subsets of features for melanoma diagnosis.
Validation method where the entire dataset is used to train and classify the same sample space to calculate f-measure.
Validation method where each lesion is removed one at a time, trained on the remaining, and classified to calculate mean f-measure.
Validation method where the dataset is split into ten parts, nine used for training and one for testing, repeated to calculate mean f-measure.
Metric used to evaluate and compare the performance of different feature subsets and diagnosing systems across Model, LOO, and 10xCV statistics.
Metrics used to evaluate and compare the performance of the proposed systems against clinical scoring systems and other automated methods.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using f-measure, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity via Model, Leave-One-Out (LOO), and 10-fold Cross-Validation (10xCV) statistics, with comparisons made based on these metric values rather than statistical significance testing.

Dermoscopy: A Tutorial Dataset (Argenzia.description: Dataset of 102 dermoscopic lesion images (52 malignant, 50 benign) used to develop and evaluate two automated melanoma diagnosing systems.

architecture_details.kernel: Polynomial degree 5 (identified as most accurate for melanoma diagnosis)
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Shape, texture, and color features (Lesion Features - LFs) extracted using active contours and methods from prior studies [15, 16, 20, 23]. Features included B (Number of abrupt segments), C (Number of colors), AM (Lesion asymmetry according to major axis), Am (Lesion asymmetry according to minor axis), E (Entropy in lesion), R (Circularity of lesion), S (Solidity of lesion), U (Uniformity in the lesion), AC (Asymmetry of color regions), and D (Dots and globules quantification).
feature_selection_methods: Support Vector Machine Recursive Feature Elimination (SVMRFE)

validation_methods: Model statistics (training and testing on same dataset)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Support Vector Machine Recursive Feature Elimination (SVMRFE)
preprocessing_techniques: Active contour (AC) segmentation to extract lesion boundaries and dots/globules
Region-based convex active contour model for automatic extraction of dots and globules
Parameter tuning of the region-based convex AC using ground truth data from 44 manually delineated images
Manual verification and blinding of automatically detected dots and globules by a general physician for 21 images
conclusions: The study developed two new automated melanoma diagnosing systems using Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers and feature selection methods (SVMRFE, Information Gain, Correlation-based Feature Subset Selection) on a dataset of 102 dermoscopic lesion images.
The first system utilized a subset of 6 lesion features (6LFs: B, C, AM, Am, E, R) selected from 10 features, achieving an f-measure of 87.25% in 10-fold cross-validation.

The second system utilized a subset of 5 lesion features (5LFs: B, C, AM, Am, D) selected from 11 features by adding a novel quantification of dots and globules, achieving the highest consistent f-measure across Model, Leave-One-Out (LOO), and 10-fold cross-validation statistics.

The 5LFs system achieved a 10-fold cross-validation accuracy of 86.9% and sensitivity of 88.68%, demonstrating superior or comparable performance to major clinical scoring systems (e.g., Menzies, 7-point, ABCD) and contemporary automated methods.

The inclusion of the novel dots and globules quantification feature improved the consistency and reliability of the diagnosing system compared to previous subsets that showed inconsistency between validation statistics.
A polynomial kernel of degree 5 was identified as the most accurate kernel for melanoma diagnosis in this context.

The study highlights that relying solely on f-measure from a single validation statistic (like 10xCV) can be misleading, as subsets with the highest f-measure in one statistic may not be the most reliable across all statistics (Model, LOO, 10xCV).
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset of only 102 dermoscopic images (52 malignant, 50 benign), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.
The dataset is sourced from a single tutorial book (Argenziano et al., 2000), raising concerns about population bias and lack of external validation on independent, diverse clinical cohorts.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; comparisons are based solely on point estimates of performance metrics.

The 'Model' statistic (training and testing on the same data) yields 100% f-measure for both systems, indicating potential overfitting or data leakage if not clearly distinguished from cross-validation results.

The authors explicitly warn that relying on a single validation statistic (e.g., 10xCV) can be misleading, as feature subsets with the highest f-measure in one statistic may be inconsistent in others (LOO or Model).

Ground truth for dots and globules was manually delineated by a general physician for only 44 images to tune parameters, while the remaining 21 images were diagnosed by the same physician without independent verification, introducing potential observer bias.

The comparison with contemporary clinical systems (Menzies, 7-points, ABCD) and automated methods uses data from other studies with different sample sizes and methodologies, making direct performance comparisons difficult.

No accuracy or f-measure is reported for the comparative method [24] in the text, limiting the ability to fully assess the relative performance of the proposed systems against that specific benchmark.
